preFix.title: Paper clay garden herb labels
preFix.subtitle: Hand-lettered plant markers made from paper clay with impressed text
preFix.excerpt: A set of herb or plant labels made from rolled paper clay, each inscribed with text using a needle tool or letter stamps and mounted on a wooden stake. Paper clay is strong enough for outdoor use when sealed. No kiln or wheel required.
preFix.sourceNotes: Paper clay garden marker technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Paper clay garden labels are one of the best introductory projects for outdoor clay work: the cellulose fibres in
make the labels much more resilient than plain air-dry clay. Sealed with an outdoor varnish, they last through a growing season.

items.name: Wooden lollipop sticks or thin wooden garden stakes

content.text: 5-6 mm thick. Cut the labels 80 mm long and 30 mm wide. For a more organic look, cut the top edge with a rounded or pointed arch rather than a straight line. While the clay is still soft, press the wooden stake into the lower quarter of each label, centred, so it penetrates about 15 mm into the clay. The stake should be embedded, not just resting on the surface.

Use the needle tool to write the plant name on the surface while the clay is still soft. Press the tool into the surface at a consistent depth and draw each letter in a single stroke. A simple printed style is easier to read than cursive in clay. Alternatively, use letter stamps pressed firmly into the surface. For stamps, place them on the clay and press evenly across all letters before lifting.

Dry flat for 24-48 hours. Once bone-dry, apply two coats of outdoor varnish or undiluted PVA to all surfaces. Allow each coat to dry fully before applying the next. The seal should cover the stake entry point on the back as well as the front. Outdoor varnish provides better weather resistance than standard PVA. A reapplication of sealant at the start of each growing season extends the life of the labels.
Press a small botanical impression (leaf or flower) beside the text while the clay is soft.
Paint the carved letters with acrylic before varnishing. This makes the text easier to read.
Make larger 120 x 50 mm labels for raised bed markers with the variety name and planting date.

rows.fix: Push stakes in while the clay is still freshly rolled. If a stake is loose in a dry label, glue in place with waterproof PVA.
rows.cause: Stake was pushed in after the clay had begun to stiffen, or the hole was too large

rows.fix: Use thinner stakes (3-4 mm) and keep the label at least 25 mm wide. Paper clay tolerates a thinner stake better than plain clay.
rows.cause: The stake diameter was too large relative to the label width, creating a stress point
rows.problem: Label cracks along the stake entry point

rows.fix: Press the needle tool deeper into the clay: 1-2 mm is more legible than a surface scratch. A V-groove letter reads better than a narrow scratch.
rows.cause: Strokes were too shallow or too fine
rows.problem: Text is difficult to read after drying

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
